experiment: fp32_residual=True vs False, iter_0 topk tie-margin
setup: Ref iter_0 weights loaded; single sample (8192 tokens) forward; measure top-8 vs top-9 score margin distribution
conclusion: fp32 residual stream does NOT widen the topk tie-margin. fraction in bf16 ambiguity is 98.89% either way.
Root cause: untrained router produces structurally narrow score distribution. With 144 experts and init_std=0.006, logits are N(0, ~0.14) → sigmoid scores are 0.5 ± 0.04, all clustered.

fp32 residual improves the *precision* of the router INPUT (x), but the score CLUSTERING is determined by the router WEIGHT INITIALIZATION. Wider margins require either (a) larger init_std (violates alignment), (b) trained router (only happens later in training), or (c) fewer experts / larger K ratio.
Therefore fp32 residual is NOT a viable mitigation for the +0.005 nat gap. The 2× memory cost of fp32 residual buys essentially nothing.
implication_for_alignment: No viable fp32-only mitigation remains. The gap is fundamentally determined by the intersection of (bf16 training + 144-expert MoE + K=8 topk). Closing it requires either a bitwise kernel match to Megatron or a model architecture change. Recommended: accept +0.005 as bf16 MoE floor.
